title: Language representation map

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Language representation map; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. canonicalExplanation.summary: A DSA concept keeps one abstract contract while implementations map operations to each language's types, libraries, ownership, and equality rules.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A dynamic sequence maps to vector in C++, ArrayList in Java, list in Python, and an explicit buffer policy in C. Your goal is to explain representation and ownership differences for one structure across two supported languages. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Greedy, backtracking and dynamic programming; Directed and weighted graph algorithms; Sequence and range problem patterns; Selected-language readiness; Frequency and lookup patterns; Priority queue applications. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A DSA concept keeps one abstract contract while implementations map operations to each language's types, libraries, ownership, and equality rules. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: Concept evidence is shared across C, C++, Java, and Python, but C pointer/lifetime safety, C++ iterator/ownership rules, Java generics/equality, and Python mutability/hashability are separate implementation facets that require evidence.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Library names can look equivalent while invalidation, mutability, overflow, recursion, and complexity guarantees differ. The draft therefore rejects: Translating syntax line by line preserves every data-structure guarantee across languages.

includes: Map conceptual arrays, nodes, maps, sets, queues, heaps and graphs to idiomatic selected-language facilities.
Explain representation and ownership differences for one structure across two supported languages.

Concept evidence is shared across C, C++, Java, and Python, but C pointer/lifetime safety, C++ iterator/ownership rules, Java generics/equality, and Python mutability/hashability are separate implementation facets that require evidence.
excludes: Library names can look equivalent while invalidation, mutability, overflow, recursion, and complexity guarantees differ.

outcomes: Explain representation and ownership differences for one structure across two supported languages.

examples.title: Language representation map: implementation transfer
examples.situation: Concept evidence is shared across C, C++, Java, and Python, but C pointer/lifetime safety, C++ iterator/ownership rules, Java generics/equality, and Python mutability/hashability are separate implementation facets that require evidence.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: Concept evidence is shared across C, C++, Java, and Python, but C pointer/lifetime safety, C++ iterator/ownership rules, Java generics/equality, and Python mutability/hashability are separate implementation facets that require evidence.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Library names can look equivalent while invalidation, mutability, overflow, recursion, and complexity guarantees differ.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.
